reference_answer: The United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit; No. 2025-1156.
Kevin Bailey Hornberger, North East, MD, pro se.
The Merit Systems Protection Board; Constance E. Travanty of the Office of the General Counsel, U.S. Merit Systems Protection Board, also represented by Katherine Michelle Smith.
Circuit Judges Dyk, Prost, and Reyna; the opinion was issued per curiam.
He worked as a Management Analyst with the licensing division of the Library of Congress's Copyright Office.
December 2, 2025.
February 2023.
The initial decision was dated October 4, 2024 (M.S.P.B.) and became final on November 8, 2024.
(1) Copyright Office termination (February 2023); (2) LOC sustains the removal; (3) Board dismisses for lack of jurisdiction (October 2024); (4) Federal Circuit affirms (December 2, 2025).

"(1) arbitrary, capricious, an abuse of discretion, or otherwise not in accordance with law; (2) obtained without procedures required by law, rule, or regulation having been followed; or (3) unsupported by substantial evidence."
Sistek v. Dep't of Veterans Affs., 955 F.3d 948, 953 (Fed. Cir. 2020).
Legal decisions are reviewed de novo and findings of fact for substantial evidence; McIntosh v. Dep't of Def., 53 F.4th 630, 638 (Fed. Cir. 2022).
Gardner v. Libr. of Cong., 774 F.2d 1081, 1082 (Fed. Cir. 1985); and Nieuwdorp v. Libr. of Cong., 872 F.2d 1000, 1002 (Fed. Cir. 1989).

For lack of jurisdiction: to appeal an adverse action an employee must generally be employed in an Executive Branch agency or the competitive service, and LOC employees work in the Legislative Branch and do not hold competitive-service positions.
"[b]ecause the [LOC] is part of the Legislative Branch, its employees are not in the competitive service . . . and may not appeal adverse actions to the [Board]."
The Board lacks authority to transfer Hornberger's appeal to another forum (5 U.S.C. § 1204).
That whatever the meaning of that decision (cited for the proposition that Government Printing Office employees may appeal an adverse action to the Board), it is not binding on the Federal Circuit.
The supplemental appendix included with the government's informal brief.
"AFFIRMED" — the court affirms the Board's dismissal of the appeal.
"No costs"; the disposition is nonprecedential ("This disposition is nonprecedential.").

He argued the Board could have heard the merits of his case instead of dismissing for lack of jurisdiction; the court rejected it because binding precedent (Gardner and Nieuwdorp) holds LOC employees are not in the competitive service and may not appeal to the Board, and Hornberger did not explain why that precedent would not apply.

The Copyright Office terminated Hornberger (February 2023); the LOC sustained the removal after reviewing an advisory decision by the Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service; Hornberger appealed to the MSPB, which dismissed for lack of jurisdiction (October 2024, final November 8, 2024); he then petitioned the Federal Circuit, which affirmed (December 2, 2025).
